segments.s364-g17: بدادندش is a past-tense plural verb (دادن) with the enclitic -ش pointing to 'him' (dative/direct object depending on verb); word order here is verb + clitic then the NP that names what was handed. آن نامه شهریار is a definite noun phrase ('that/the letter of the king') where آن is a demonstrative determiner and شهریار is the possessor/title following the noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.s364-g18: سرآهنگ مردان نیزه گزار is a sequence of nominal modifiers: سرآهنگ 'chief/leader' modifies the following group, and مردان نیزه گزار is a compound describing the group (mardan 'men' + 'nīze-guzār' = spear‑bearers). Poetic Persian often stacks nouns without an explicit ezāfe; the whole phrase functions as an appositive identifying whose letter it was or who is meant.
segments.s364-g19: دبیرش is 'his scribe/secretary' with the possessive enclitic -ش ('his'); the proper name مران follows in apposition (his scribe, Marān). نامه را shows the definite object marked by the enclitic را, and برگشاد is the past participle/verb 'opened' placed after the object (typical SOV order in Persian, here in a poetic register).
segments.s364-g20: بخواندش is the past-tense verb 'he read' with enclitic -ش meaning 'to him' (dative: 'read it to him'). بران is a deictic/adverbial 'to/at that' linking to the following noun phrase; شاه جادو نژاد is 'king, magic-born' (جادو 'magic' + نژاد 'race/lineage' forming a compound epithet), so the verb phrase means 'read it to that magic‑born king.'
segments.s364-g21: نوشته here is a nominal 'the writing/text' (fronted), followed by دران نامه شهریار 'in that letter of the king' — دران is an archaic/poetic contraction meaning 'in that' (در + آن). The whole phrase introduces the content of what was written.
segments.s364-g22: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/about/concerning' (here 'concerning' or 'about'), followed by a coordinated list گردان و مردان نیزه گزار 'troops and the spear‑bearing men'. As before, نیزه گزار is a compound 'spear-bearers' and the coordination lacks an additional particle because of the terse poetic style.
segments.s364-g23: پس is a narrative adverb 'then/thereupon.' The sequence شاه لهراسپ گشتاسپ شاه lists royal names/titles in apposition: 'the king Lohrasp, (and) Gushtasp, king' — repetition/stacking of names and the title is a poetic device naming the ruler(s).
segments.s364-g24: نگهبان گیتی is a genitive phrase 'guardian/protector of the world' (نگهبان + گیتی). سزاوار گاه places the adjective/synonym 'worthy/deserving' before گاه ('place/time' used poetically for 'throne' or 'seat'), so together it reads as an epithet: 'guardian of the world, deserving of the throne.'
segments.s364-g113: بران سان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'thus/on that manner' (بران = 'on that', سان = 'so/thus'). همی رفت is the poetic/progressive past (همی + past verb) meaning 'he went' or 'kept going'; بایین is the adverb 'down'. خشم here names the state in which he went (literally 'anger'), functioning adverbially: 'went down in anger'. Word order is poetic (adverbial phrase + progressive past + adverb + bare noun-state).
segments.s364-g114: پر از خون شده دل is a verbless/nominal clause arranged as 'full of blood become heart' = 'the heart became full of blood': پر از خون is the predicative phrase 'full of blood', شده is 'became/has become', دل is the noun linked as the subject. The second half, پر از آب چشم, repeats the same construction elliptically (verb omitted): 'eyes full of water' (i.e. tears). This paratactic repetition is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s364-g115: همی کرد غارت uses همی + کرد (past) to give a continuous/iterative sense: 'he kept doing plunder' = 'he plundered repeatedly/continuously.' غارت is the verbal noun 'plunder' functioning as the object of کرد (i.e. 'did plunder'). The order (adverbial marker + verb + noun-object) is typical of classical poetry.
segments.s364-g116: همی سوخت کاخ has همی marking the ongoing action and سوخت (past of سوختن) 'burned'. کاخ (palace) follows the verb as the affected thing/subject of the burning; English requires 'the palace burned' or 'the palace was burning.'
segments.s364-g117: در آورد is a compound verbal phrase ('در' + آورد) here meaning 'brought into/caused to enter'; آوَرد is past tense 'he brought'. لشکر is the thing brought ('an army') and appears before the verb (word order is flexible in poetry). به ایران‌زمین is the directional prepositional phrase 'into the land of Iran' marking destination.
segments.s364-g118: همه خیره و دل پراگنده کین: همه 'everyone/all' + خیره 'staring/astonished' describes the crowd's reaction. و links to a second nominal clause: دل (hearts) + پراگنده (scattered, dispersed) with کین ('hatred/vengeance') specifying the content or cause — literally 'hearts scattered with hatred.' Possessive pronouns are omitted (their hearts) as is common in Persian epic diction.
segments.s364-g41: تو is the explicit 2nd-person singular subject. بر خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'upon yourself' (بر = on/upon + خویشتن = self). The second بر appears as a separable preverb with the verb میفزای (a poetic/present form of افزودن), so بر ... میفزای = 'add upon'; میفزای is 2nd-singular present indicative (you add/you increase). رنج is the direct object 'hardship/suffering.'
segments.s364-g42: که introduces a causal clause here ('for/because'). ما is the 1st‑person plural subject. بر here combines with the verb گشادیم to form برگشودن (a compound verb meaning 'to open up'); گشادیم is first‑person plural past/perfect 'we opened/threw open.' درهای is the plural construct 'doors of' (در = door, -های plural marker attached), and گنج is the genitive head 'treasure.'
segments.s364-g43: بیارم is an older/poetic first‑person singular of آوردن meaning 'I will bring' / 'I bring' (optative/intentional sense in poetry). ز + noun marks source 'from the troop(s)'; گردان here means 'troop / company.' هزاران هزار is a poetic intensifier 'thousands and thousands' (myriads).
segments.s364-g44: همه at the start of the phrase means 'all / every one.' کار دیده is a compound meaning 'experienced in deeds' (کار = deed/affair, دیده = seen/past participle → 'seen/seasoned'); the phrase describes their experience. The second همه again 'all,' and نیزه‌دار is a compound noun 'spear‑bearer' (نیزه = spear + دار = holder), describing their armament.
segments.s364-g45: همه 'all/everyone.' ایرجی زاده is a patronymic/lineage phrase: ایرجی (of Irāj) + زاده 'born of / descendant,' i.e. 'sons/descendants of Irāj.' و simply links the two lineage markers. پهلوی here names the Pahlavi/Pahlav affiliation (an ethnico‑dynastic identifier); it functions as a second attribute of origin.
segments.s364-g46: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative construction 'neither ... nor ...'. افراسیابی names 'of Afrasiab' (i.e. belonging to Afrasiab) and is negated by نه. یبغوی here is presented as the second item negated by نه; as a lexical item in the lemma list it does not fit the given verb senses, so it is best read as a proper name/title (Yabghu) in this context.
segments.s364-g47: همه 'all.' شاه چهر is a poetic compound: شاه (king/royal) + چهر (face/appearance) = 'kingly-faced' or 'of royal aspect.' و links to the parallel epithet همه ماه روی where ماه ('moon') is used metaphorically for beauty and روی = 'face' giving 'moon‑faced' or 'moon‑bright countenance.' Word order is adjective‑noun in poetry, and both phrases are parallel epithets describing appearance.
segments.s364-g48: همه again 'all.' سرو بالا is a stereotyped simile: سرو (cypress) used for tall, graceful stature and بالا (high/lofty) qualifies it — together meaning 'tall as cypresses.' The second همه and راست‌گوی (راست + گو 'say/one who speaks') form the adjective 'truthful/honest' describing their character. The two halves parallel appearance and character.
segments.s364-g89: بدیشان (به ایشان) is a dative phrase meaning 'to them'; it precedes the verb as the indirect object. ببخشید is the past third‑person singular of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'bestowed/gave' (classical/past narrative form). Together they mean 'he gave to them...'.
segments.s364-g90: This is a compact noun phrase describing what was given. گوان here functions as a poetic connective/quantifier attached to the numeral phrase; گزیده is an adjective/participle 'chosen/elite' modifying the head noun سوار ('horsemen'), which comes last. نبرده is a negative perfective participle 'not taken/has not been taken' placed before سوار to qualify those horsemen as 'not captured/undefeated'. Word order (modifier ... head) is typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s364-g91: در گنج is a locative phrase 'in/inside the treasury' (در + گنج). بگشاد is a past narrative form (classical) from گشودن meaning 'opened'. و links the two clauses; روزی بداد is an idiomatic verbless‑looking phrase where روزی ('provision/sustenance' here) is the object and بداد (past of دادن) means 'he gave'—together 'he gave provisions'.
segments.s364-g92: This clause continues with three verbs/nominals in sequence. بزد is the past of زدن 'he struck/struck'. نای رویین is a noun phrase 'an iron (رویین) reed/pipe' (نای used metaphorically or of a metal pipe), with the adjective following the noun in classical order. بنه بر نهاد is a light‑verb construction: بنه (a small heap/bundle) is the object and نهاد (past of نهادن) is 'he placed/put upon'—literally 'he struck the iron reed and placed a heap on it.'
segments.s364-g93: بخواند is the past tense here from خواندن used in the sense 'he summoned/called'. آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' modifying the verb. مر is an enclitic dative 'to me' (classical form of 'مرا'/'به من') and برادرش را is the definite object 'his brother' (برادر + possessive ش + object marker را). The sequence indicates 'he summoned, at that time, my (or to me) his brother' — idiomatically: 'he summoned my brother.'
segments.s364-g94: بدو (به او/بهش) is 'to him'; داد is past 'gave'. یک دست لشکرش را is a unit phrase: یک دست 'one hand' here idiomatically means 'one contingent/division', لشکرش 'his army' with possessive ش, and را marks the phrase as definite object. So 'to him he gave one contingent of his army.' Word order places the indirect object (بدو) before the verb and the direct object after it.
segments.s364-g95: باندیدمان is a past form meaning 'he bound/secured us' (compound with the object enclitic مان 'us'). داد again is 'gave'. دست دگر 'another hand/contingent' (دست = 'hand' used figuratively for a military detachment; دگر = 'other/another') is the direct object of داد. The clause says he secured one party and gave another detachment.
segments.s364-g96: خود is an intensive/reflexive 'he himself'. اندر میان رفت is a verbal phrase 'went into the midst' (اندر + میان = 'into the middle', رفت = 'went'). با یک پسر is a prepositional phrase 'with one son' (با = with, یک = one, پسر = son/youth). The whole means 'he himself went into the middle with one son.'
segments.s364-g33: شنیدیم و دید is a simple compound past: two past-tense verbs joined by و ('and') meaning 'we heard and saw.' آن سخنها is the direct object 'those words' and کجا ('where') follows it to indicate the location/circumstance observed. Word order is typical poetic Persian: verb(s) + object + locative adverb.
segments.s364-g34: نبودی is the past negative of بودن ('you were not'); تو is the emphatic subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion). مر ('to me') is an indirect object clitic standing before the verbal noun گفتنش ('the telling of it'); را marks گفتنش as the definite object; سزا functions as the predicate meaning 'worthy/deserving.' The whole phrase means 'you were not worthy of telling it to me.'
segments.s364-g35: The repeated نه ... و نه construction gives a series of negated participles. پوشیدنی and بنمودنی are verbal-adjective/gerundial forms (-نی) meaning 'that can be covered/hidden' and 'that can be shown/displayed' respectively; both are negated here ('not to be hidden, nor to be shown').
segments.s364-g36: A continuation of the same negating pattern. افگندنی is a gerundial/adjectival form of افکندن ('to cast/throw') and پیسودنی is another -نی form (here meaning 'to be pleasing / to be praised'); both are negated by نه, completing a fourfold list of things that are not appropriate.
segments.s364-g37: چنان گفته بودی is a past perfect / pluperfect: 'you had said such (things).' Here گفته is the past participle and بودی functions as the auxiliary forming the pluperfect. که introduces the content clause; من is the subject of the following clause and تا دو ماه gives the temporal limit 'for/up to two months' (تا used here as a temporal 'until/for').
segments.s364-g38: سوی is a preposition 'toward/to,' followed by the noun phrase کشور خرم ('the joyful/pleasant country'). آرم is an archaic/poetic first-person verb form meaning 'I will bring / I shall lead' (classical contraction of آورم), and سپاه is the direct object 'army, host.' The verb appears in first person to report the speaker's promised action.
segments.s364-g39: This clause uses poetic negation and elliptical numbering. نه دو ماه begins the negation 'not two months'; باید is the modal 'it must / it takes,' here negated by the surrounding phrasing; ز تو means 'from you' (i.e. on your part); نی is an archaic/poetic negative particle ('not'). The sense is rhetorical: it will not take two months (indeed not even four) on your part.
segments.s364-g40: کجا here is a rhetorical/interrogative 'where/how' introducing the question; من is the subject and بیایم is the present/subjunctive 'should I come / may I come' (used rhetorically). چو is a simile 'as/like,' شیر is 'lion,' and شکار is 'hunt' — together 'like a lion on the hunt.'
segments.s364-g97: This nominal phrase strings descriptive adjectives after the head noun in typical Persian order: 'یکی' ('a certain') introduces an indefinite person, 'ترک' is the ethnic noun 'Turk' (the head), followed by the compound adjective 'بد نام' ('infamous', literally 'bad‑named') and the epithet 'گرگسار' ('wolf‑like' or 'ferocious'). Together they form ‘a certain infamous, wolf‑like Turk.’
segments.s364-g98: 'گذشته' is a past participial/adverbial form meaning 'having gone' or simply 'gone/passed'; 'بروبر' here functions adverbially as 'straight on/forward' or 'directly'; 'بسی روزگار' is a time‑phrase, literally 'much time' = 'many days'. The whole phrase modifies the preceding noun: he had passed straight on for many days.
segments.s364-g99: 'سپه را' is the direct object 'the army' plus the Persian object marker 'را'; 'بدو' is the dative clitic meaning 'to him'; 'داد' is the past verb 'gave'. 'اسپهبدی' names the office/title ('as spahbed' or 'the spahbedship') and here is used as a complement showing what was given — i.e. command of the army.
segments.s364-g100: This clause consists of the address or topic 'تو' ('you') followed by 'گفتی' (literally 'you said' or a reportive verb; the provided lexical senses do not match the verbal use, so it is treated as a reporting verb). The subordinate clause 'نداند همی جز بدی' uses 'نداند' (present/habitual negative of 'to know' here meaning 'know' or 'be aware'), with the continuous/intensifying particle 'همی' and 'جز' ('except') plus 'بدی' ('evil') — together: '(you) said (that) he knows nothing except evil.'
segments.s364-g101: 'چو' is the subordinating temporal conjunction 'when'; 'غارتگری' (literally 'pillage/raiding') names the raider or the act of raiding; 'داد' is the past verb 'gave' (here part of the narrative action); 'بر' is the preposition 'against/on/upon'; 'بیدرفش' is a compounded adjective/noun meaning 'without banner' or 'bannerless' and serves as the object/target of the prepositional phrase — so: 'when a raider struck at the bannerless...'.
segments.s364-g102: The verb phrase 'بدادش' functions here as 'he gave him' (an archaic or poetic verbal form meaning 'gave to him'); 'یکی' introduces the item as 'a certain' or 'one'; 'پیل پیکر' is a compound attributive: 'پیل' ('elephant') + 'پیکر' ('bodied/large‑bodied') = 'elephant‑sized/elephant‑bodied'; 'درفش' is 'standard/banner'. The full segment: 'he gave him an elephant‑sized standard.'
segments.s364-g103: 'یکی بود' is the common narrative formula 'there was one'; 'نامش' = 'his name' (name + enclitic 'ش' = 'his'); 'خشاش' is here a proper name (used as the person's name); 'دلیر' is an appositive adjective meaning 'brave'. Word order: determiner + copula + name + epithet = 'There was one whose name was Khashash, the brave.'
segments.s364-g104: 'پذیره' is used as 'refuge/acceptance' (a noun meaning shelter or reception); 'نرفتی' is a negative past form 'did not go/was not taken' (here idiomatically 'would not receive/accept him'); 'ورا' = 'to him' (dative); 'نره شیر' is a compound 'male lion' ('نره' = male, 'شیر' = lion) used emphatically to show even a lion would not go to him. Together the clause says no refuge would accept him — not even a male lion approached.
segments.s364-g49: همه here functions as a distributive/plural marker meaning 'some/all of them' (literary usage). از + در takes the sense 'from the door/gate of…' so از در پادشاهی means 'from the gate/house of kingship' (origin/association). گاه at the end is the adverb 'occasionally/sometimes' qualifying the preceding phrase.
segments.s364-g50: This clause mirrors the previous structure: از در + noun indicates origin 'from the gate/house of…'; گنج here is 'treasure-house/vault' rather than an abstract 'treasure'. The final و کلاه strings on another alternative (kinds of people) — کلاه is literal 'cap/hat'.
segments.s364-g51: جهانشان is the possessed noun 'their world/their life' (noun + ـشان). بفرسوده is a poetic past participle meaning 'worn/consumed' (ب- prefix + فرسوده). با introduces the instruments/causes 'by/with', so با رنج و ناز means 'by suffering and by pampering/indulgence'.
segments.s364-g52: Repeated همه marks the whole group. شیرگیر is an agent noun 'lion-catcher' (brave fighter in epic usage). سرفراز is an adjective/noun meaning 'proud/victorious'; the clause lists heroic qualities in parallel.
segments.s364-g53: نیزه‌داران is the plural agent noun 'spear-bearers' (نیزه + دار + ان). شمشیر زن is a compound: شمشیر 'sword' + زن (here an agentive/verb sense 'one who strikes') = 'swordsman' or 'sword-striker'. The word order is noun + agent element, typical for compound warrior-epithets.
segments.s364-g54: همه again marks the whole group. باره‌انگیز is an adjective 'stirring/rousing' (causing a charge or excitement). لشکر شکن is a compound 'army (لشکر) + breaker (شکن)' = 'breaker of hosts/armies'. The two adjectives are joined by و.
segments.s364-g55: چو is the conjunction 'when'. دانند is present-tense plural 'they know/ perceive/recognize' (literary form). کم here means 'a few' and qualifies کوس 'war-drum/drumbeat'. بر + پیل means 'upon/on the elephant'; بست is the past participle/verb 'bound/fastened' (here likely 'fastened/affixed' or 'struck onto'), so the clause says 'when they know/see/hear that a few drums are on/attached to the elephant'.
segments.s364-g56: سم اسپ is a genitive/compound 'the hoof (سم) of the steed (اسپ)'. ایشان is 'their' or 'them' (third-person plural possessive/pronoun). کند is the present-tense verb 'makes/does' (here causative: 'makes [the mountain] low'). کوه پست is the object phrase 'the mountain low' (پست = low). The clause means their horse's hoof levels the mountain.
segments.s364-g73: به‌روز نبرد is a temporal phrase meaning 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز = 'of the day' modifying نبرد = 'battle'). ار is the archaic conditional 'if'. بخواهد is a third-person present/future-form verb 'wills/cares' (classical order often places the subject after the verb), and خدای here is the subject 'God' following the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s364-g74: به رزم اندر آرم is a goal + verb phrase: به (towards/into) + رزم (battle) + اندر (in/within) + آرم (first-person verb, 'I place/lay/establish' in an older poetic sense). The object phrase سرت زیر پای ('your head under [the] foot') names what is placed underfoot; word order and the possessive sense (my foot) are implied rather than expressed.
segments.s364-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. سالار is the subject 'the commander'. پیکند is an archaic/past-form verb meaning 'sent a messenger' (classical diction). نامه بخواند is a past action 'the letter was read' or 'he read the letter' (بخواند is classical past/recitative form). The two verb phrases form a temporal clause ('when X sent a messenger / the letter was read').
segments.s364-g76: فرود آمد is a compound past tense 'he descended/ alighted' (فرود = descent/alighting + آمد = came). از گاه = 'from (his) place/seat' (گاه = place/time). و is the coordinating 'and'. خیره بماند is a classical past/result phrase 'remained astonished/staring' (خیره = astonished/staring; بماند = remained).
segments.s364-g77: سپهبدش را گفت literally 'he said to his spahbed' — را marks the indirect object (the person addressed). سپهبدش is 'his spahbed' (possessive -ش). گفت is the past verb 'said/told' and فردا پگاه is the time phrase 'tomorrow at dawn' placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s364-g78: بخوان here functions as an imperative/instruction ('summon/bring together' in this context; classically the same form can mean 'read' or 'call'). از همه پادشاهی سپاه literally 'the army/armies from all the kingdom' (از = from; همه پادشاهی = the whole kingdom; سپاه = army). Word order omits an explicit ezāfe and is compressed for terse command.
segments.s364-g79: This cluster lists forces in apposition: تگینان (plural title 'Tegins') labels certain leaders, لشکرش is 'his army' (possessive suffix -ش), and ترکان چین is a noun‑noun compound 'the Turks of China' (modifier follows the head in classical style). The sequence names who marched.
segments.s364-g80: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they departed/advanced'. هر سو is an adverbial phrase 'in every direction' (هر = every; سو = side/toward). به توران زمین = 'toward the land of Turan' (به = toward/into; توران = Turan; زمین = land/country).
segments.s364-g65: به خورشید مانند is a simile: به + noun + مانند = “like / in the manner of the sun.” Here the prepositional element به is used idiomatically with مانند; سپس با introduces accompaniment (با تاج و تخت = “with crown and throne”), where با marks what accompanies or adorns the subject. Word order is emphatic/poetic (simile phrase followed by the accompanying items).
segments.s364-g66: همی is a habitual/adverbial particle meaning “continually / always” placed before the verb; تابد (from تافتن) is an older present- or stative-verb form meaning “shine.” از introduces the source (“from their spears”); نیزه‌شان is a noun with the possessive enclitic -شان. فر و بخت are coordinated nouns (‘glory and fortune’) and function as the logical subject whose action is expressed by تابد (poetic agreement keeps the older singular verb form).
segments.s364-g67: چنینم is an adverbial particle meaning “thus / so” (the -م is an older clitic); گوانند is an archaic plural verb ‘they say’ (گفتن family). The coordinating و + اسپهبدان attaches the group named (the spahbeds) as part of those who utter this statement — poetic, somewhat discontinuous word order.
segments.s364-g68: گزین and پسندیده are adjectival/participial modifiers meaning ‘chosen/select’ and ‘approved/pleasing,’ placed before the head noun موبدان (the priests). Here گزین functions as a prepositive adjective (‘select/chosen’), and the two adjectives are coordinated with و to qualify موبدان.
segments.s364-g69: This clause is elliptical (the verb ‘make’ or ‘set’ is implied): تو is the addressed subject ‘you’; سیحون مینبار is a noun + appositive/predicative (Sihon treated as a ‘pulpit’), and و connects the parallel phrase جیحون به مشک where به + مشک marks Jayhun as endowed/charged ‘with musk’ (i.e. ‘fragrant with musk’). The structure lists two grand images of rivers as if transformed by the addressee.
segments.s364-g70: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause (‘for us’ / ‘as for us’); ما را uses را in a dative/topic-like function (‘to us’ / ‘for us’). The repeated چه … چه … construction is correlative/indefinite (‘whether … or …’). خشک here is an adjective ‘dry,’ and the whole clause means essentially “what does it matter to us whether (it is) Jayhun or Sihon, or (even) dry.”
segments.s364-g71: چنان is an adverb (‘thus / so’) modifying the verb بردوانند (an older plural form meaning ‘they set/drive/carry’). باره is the direct object ‘load / burden’ and بر آب is the prepositional phrase ‘upon/on the water’ indicating where the loads are set. Word order (adverb – verb – object – prepositional phrase) is typical of poetic prose.
segments.s364-g72: که introduces a result or consequence clause (‘so that / such that’). تاری here is the adjectival/noun idea ‘darkness / dark’ and is predicated by شود (شدن = ‘become’), so that the subject چشمه آفتاب (‘the spring / source of the sun’) is said to ‘become dark’ — a poetic image of the sun’s source being dimmed as a result.
segments.s364-g57: ازیشان is از + ایشان and here functions partitive/partitive-from: 'from them, of them'. The phrase that follows is a noun phrase introduced by the numeral دو ('two') + گرد (an archaic/epic unit = band/company), with گزیده as a postpositive/attributive adjective meaning 'chosen/elite' modifying سوار 'horsemen'. Word order is partitive phrase + numeral + unit + adjective + head noun.
segments.s364-g58: This is a simple nominal apposition/listing: a proper name (زریر) followed by a title (سپهدار) which post-modifies the name, then 'و' linking the second name (اسفندیار). The title after the name is common in epic style.
segments.s364-g59: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ایشان is the 3pl pronoun 'they'. بپوشند is 3pl present/subjunctive of پوشیدن 'to put on / wear' (= 'they put on'). ز + آهن expresses material 'of iron' (poetic/prepositional use), and قبای is 'robe' (قبا) governed by that phrase: 'robes made of iron'.
segments.s364-g60: به here marks direction/target ('toward' or 'to'), followed by خورشید و ماه 'sun and moon'. اندرآرند is 3pl from اندرآوردن/انداختن 'to bring/put in', used here as 'they place/bring' and پای 'foot' is the object (collectively 'they set their feet toward the sun and moon') — a hyperbolic image. Word order: directional phrase + verb + object.
segments.s364-g61: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. بر گردن ('on/over the neck') is the prepositional phrase expressing where something is placed. آرند (3pl of آوردن/آرد in this poetic form) means 'they place/bring'. رخشنده is an attributive adjective 'radiant/shining' modifying گرز 'mace'; adjective precedes the noun here.
segments.s364-g62: همی تابد is a frequentative/perfective-present idiom 'continually/ever it shines' (همی + present verb). از marks source 'from', گرزشان is گرز + possessive suffix 'their mace(s)'. فر و برز are two abstract nouns 'glory/splendour and highness/exaltation' produced as the effect/source of the mace; the coordinate 'و' links them.
segments.s364-g63: چو introduces 'when'. ایشان 'they' is subject. بباشند is the 3pl present/subjunctive form of بودن/باشیدن here meaning 'they are/stand' (archaic/epic verbal form). پیش سپاه = 'in the presence of / before the host' (preposition + noun).
segments.s364-g64: ترا here stands for 'to you' (the 2nd-person clitic + را), marking the person addressed. کرد + باید form an idiomatic necessity construction: کرد (do/make) with باید ('must') yields 'it is necessary (for you) to ...' or more simply 'you must'. بدیشان is the dative 'to them/for them' and نگاه 'gaze/watch' is a noun used with کردن (here inverted order) to mean 'to watch/keep an eye on'. The whole string yields 'you must keep watch over them.'
segments.s364-g81: بدو is the prepositional/clitic form meaning “to him/unto him” placed before the verb. باز is an adverb meaning “back/again” modifying the verb. خواندند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (they called/summoned) from خواندن; it is used here in the sense “to call/summon.” لشکرش را is the definite object: لشکر (army/host) with the possessive clitic ش “his” and the object marker را; the whole noun phrase is the object of خواندند.
segments.s364-g82: سر is used as ‘chief/leader’ and heads the noun phrase سرِ مرزداران (the leaders of the border-guards). مرزداران is the plural noun “border-guards.” کشورش is a possessive chain: کشور (country/realm) + ش “his.” The final را marks the entire noun phrase as definite/object (the thing called back). Classical Persian allows this stacking of nouns (head + modifier + possessor + را).
segments.s364-g83: برادر بد او is a noun phrase ‘his wicked/evil brother’ (برادر = brother; بد = bad/evil; او = he/his). The enclitic را after او marks the phrase as definite/topic. دو آهرمنان that follows is an appositive describing that brother: ‘two Ahrimans’ (آهرمنان plural). In this classical/poetic style the apposition follows the marked noun phrase.
segments.s364-g84: یکی is the numeral/pronoun ‘one (of them)’ and دیگری ‘the other’. The copula/verb is omitted (typical in Persian appositive/equative lines), so the line reads ‘one (was) Kahrm and the other (was) Andaman.’ کهرم and اندمان here function as proper names (the two Ahrimans), so this is an equative identification.
segments.s364-g85: بفرمودشان is past tense (honorific/causative) ‘he commanded/ordered them’; the attached شان is the clitic object ‘them’. تا here introduces the content/purpose of the order (so that/that). نبرده is the negative past participle ‘not-taken/has not taken’ used attributively, and سوار is the noun ‘riders/horsemen’ — together meaning ‘horsemen who had not taken (spoil)’ (classical style often uses a participle before the noun it modifies).
segments.s364-g86: گزیدند is 3rd-person plural past ‘they chose/selected.’ گردان لشکر هزار is a stacked noun phrase meaning a division/unit of the army of a thousand (literally ‘a troop/division, army, thousand’); Persian allows such noun stacking where گردان (troop/division) is specified by لشکر (army) and the numeral هزار (thousand) quantifies the unit. The noun phrase is the object of گزیدند.
segments.s364-g87: بدادندشان is past 3rd-person plural ‘they gave’, with the clitic شان marking the indirect object ‘them’. The direct objects follow in a series (کوس و پیل و درفش): کوس (war-drum), پیل (elephant), درفش (banner). The verb precedes its objects here (poetic inversion), and the objects are listed without individual را markers.
segments.s364-g88: بیاراسته is an adjectival/participle phrase qualifying the preceding items (meaning here ‘adorned/decorated’ in context; the copula is omitted). It is followed by a color list (زرد و سرخ و بنفش) that specifies the decoration: زرد ‘yellow’, سرخ ‘red’, بنفش ‘purple’. In classical Persian adjectives or participles may appear before the color terms they govern.
segments.s364-g9: چینش: 'از ایران' sets the origin ('from Iran'), followed by 'فرخ' used as a predicative adjective meaning "fortunate/happy". The construction 'به + [noun] + شدن' is idiomatic here: 'به خلخ شدند' means 'were put into (or became) ankleted' — i.e. they came to be wearing anklets. The past plural verb شدند agrees with the (implied) plural subject.
segments.s364-g10: ولیکن introduces a contrast ('but'). 'به خلخ' repeats the 'into/into anklets' sense and 'نه فرخ شدند' places نه (not) before the adjective phrase to negate becoming 'فرخ' — 'they did not become fortunate.' The verb شدند is again past plural, and the line deliberately contrasts the outward mark (anklets) with the lack of inner fortune.
segments.s364-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'از دور' ('from afar') modifies دیدند (past, third-person plural 'they saw'). The direct object follows the verb in poetic order: 'ایوان شاه' = 'the king's iwan/hall.'
segments.s364-g12: زده is a past participle of زدن used here in the sense 'hoisted/raised' (literally 'struck/placed'); 'بر سر او' is a prepositional phrase 'over/above his head'; 'درفش سیاه' is a noun+adjective phrase 'black banner/standard'. Together this describes the state they saw: a black standard hoisted above him.
segments.s364-g13: فرود آمدند is the compound past tense 'they alighted / dismounted' (آمدند after فرود). 'از' marks source ('from'), and 'چمنده ستور' is a compound referring to the beasts' packs/saddles — i.e. 'from the pack-saddles/pack-beasts' (they got down from their loaded animals).
segments.s364-g14: شکسته‌دل is a compound adjective 'broken‑hearted' describing the (implied) subject. The coordiner و links that state to 'چشمها گشته کور' — 'their eyes had become blind.' گشته is the perfect participle/verb 'become/turned' and کور is the resulting adjective 'blind'; the possessive ('their') is implicit from the earlier subject.
segments.s364-g15: پیاده is an adverb/adjective 'on foot'; برفتند is an older/poetic past 'they went/proceeded'. تا here means 'as far as/up to', and پیش اوی is a prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence' using the archaic form اوی for 'him'.
segments.s364-g16: سیه‌شان شده جامه literally reads with the possessive suffix -شان attached to the adjective: 'their garments had become black' (سیه = black, شده = become, جامه = garment). The second part 'و زرد روی' means 'and (with) yellow/pale face' — زرد (pale) qualifying روی (face). The sequence describes the altered clothing and visage as a result of their plight.
segments.s364-g25: The verb فرستاد is simple past 'sent' and the usual subject خدای (God) appears after the verb for poetic inversion; فرسته is the direct object 'a messenger'. زی او is an older prepositional/agent-like phrase attached to the verb phrase (here functioning effectively as 'to/for him' in context) and او is the third-person pronoun 'him'.
segments.s364-g26: همه مهتران is a nominal phrase 'all the elders/great ones' (مهتران = leading/older men); پیش او = 'before him / in his presence' (پیش used as 'in the presence of'). بر به پای is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'on foot' (بر + به + پای = literally 'upon the foot').
segments.s364-g27: زی here is the old prepositional particle 'from/of' introducing the source (زی ارجاسپ ترک = 'from Arjasp the Turk'). آن پلید سترگ is a compact epithet: آن پلید 'that vile one' and سترگ (literally 'eye') used adjectivally as 'large‑eyed' or 'with a foul gaze'.
segments.s364-g28: کجا begins a relative/poetic clause 'whose/where' (here functioning like 'whose'); پیکرش پیکر is a tautological poetic doubling 'his body — the body' used for emphasis, followed by پیر گرگ 'old wolf' describing that form. Together they mean 'whose form was that of an old wolf.'
segments.s364-g29: زده سر ز is an idiomatic verbless or participial construction meaning 'having the head struck away / having turned his head away from...' — here 'زده' (affected/struck) + 'سر' (head) + ز (from) form 'turned away from'. آیین و دین بهی = 'the good rites and religion' (بهی = 'good' / 'of the Good Religion').
segments.s364-g30: گزینه ره is a nominal-predicative phrase 'having chosen the way' (گزینه here functions as 'chosen/choice' and pairs with ره 'way'); کوری و ابلهی = 'blindness and folly' specify the path chosen. The subject is implied from the prior clause.
segments.s364-g31: رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached'; آن نوشته فرومایه‌وار is the subject 'that base/petty writing/letter' placed after the verb for poetic effect. فرومایه‌وار is an adverbial/adjectival modifier meaning 'lowly/basel y' describing the letter.
segments.s364-g32: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; بنوشته is a past participle 'written' and بودی functions here as an auxiliary in the past ('you had written' or 'you wrote') — بنوشته بودی سوی شهریار = 'which you had written to the king' (سوی used as 'to/toward').
segments.s364-g105: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. دیده‌بان کردش is a verbal clause where کرد (to make/do) takes the enclitic object -ش ('him') — so 'made him a lookout/sentry'. The following و پیش رو is an adverbial phrase ('and (placed) forward/in front') with پیش + رو functioning together to indicate position.
segments.s364-g106: کشیدش is کشید (pulled/hoisted) with the enclitic -ش ('him/it'); in this epic context it means 'hoisted/drew up' (the standard or made him take a role). درفش names that standard. The classical past بَشد (بشد) = 'it became / he became' introduces the result 'پیش‌گو' (soothsayer); ب‑ is an older verbal prefix common in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s364-g107: دگر ترک 'another Turk' (دگر = another). بد نام is a split adjective phrase 'bad-named' = 'ill-reputed / notorious' modifying ترک. او هوش دیو is an appositive description: او (he) + هوش (mind/intelligence) + دیو (demon) — shorthand for 'he had a devilish mind' (copula omitted, as common in poetry).
segments.s364-g108: پیامش is پیام (message) with the possessive enclitic -ش 'his message'. فرستاد is the past tense 'sent'. ترکان خدیو is a compressed/poetic phrase meaning 'the khedive of the Turks' (the indirect recipient/authority); the preposition 'to' is omitted in poetry and understood from context.
segments.s364-g109: نگه‌دار گفتا: نگه‌دار is the guardian/keeper and گفتا is the classical past 'said' (Ferdowsi uses archaic -ا ending). The sequence تو پشت سپاه is an address + location: 'you — at the rear (پشت) of the army (سپاه)'.
segments.s364-g110: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. از ما کسی is 'anyone of us' (از marking partitive/of). باز گردد uses باز ('back/again') + گردد (classical form of گردد/گشتن) meaning 'turns back' — here a subjunctive/conditional sense. به راه is the directional phrase 'onto the road/path'.
segments.s364-g111: هم آنجا که بینی = 'right there where you see (him)' — هم adds emphasis, آنجا 'there', که introduces the relative clause, بینی is 2nd‑person present 'you see'. مر او را بکش = مر (to me/for me) + او را (him, definite object) + بکش (imperative 'kill!') — 'kill him for me.'
segments.s364-g112: نگر is an imperative 'look/watch'. تا here gives purpose/result 'so that / in order that'. بدانجا 'to that place/that far'. نجنبدت is negative jussive/imperative 'do not move/stir' with the second‑person clitic -ت. هش is an interjection 'beware!' — a final warning.
segments.s364-g1: سخن is the subject 'the speech'. چون here is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when/as'. بسر برد is a fixed, poetic perfective phrase meaning 'came to an end' (بسر = pass/time; برد used as the past verb), so the clause reads literally 'when the speech came to an end.' Word order places the temporal conjunction before the compound verb.
segments.s364-g2: سیه پیل را is a direct-object phrase: سیه 'black' modifies پیل 'elephant', with را marking it definite/accusative. خواند here is not 'read' but 'called/summoned' (used transitively). و کرد آفرین is a two-word clause where کرد (past of کردن) functions as 'uttered/said' and آفرین is the acclamation; this is a common poetic sequence meaning 'he exclaimed “Bravo.”'
segments.s364-g3: سپردش is a past transitive verb + clitic (سپرد + ش) meaning 'he handed/entrusted him/it'. بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him'. گفت here introduces reported speech ('he said'), followed by the quoted imperative بردارشان: بردار (imperative 'take') + شان (object 'them'). The sequence is 'he entrusted him and said: "Take them."'
segments.s364-g4: از ایران به آن مرز is a prepositional frame giving source and destination ('from Iran to that border'). بگذارشان is the imperative/optative from گذاشتن with the clitic -شان 'them' and here means 'put/leave them (there)'. In context it gives the destination command: 'take them from Iran and leave them at that border.'
segments.s364-g5: فرستادگان is the plural noun 'envoys' (agents sent). سپهدار چین is a title + place: سپهدار 'commander' qualified by چین 'China', i.e. 'the Chinese commander' or 'commander of China'. The whole noun phrase means 'the envoys of the Chinese commander.'
segments.s364-g6: ز پیش is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from the presence of' or 'from before'. جهانجوی is an epithet 'world-seeking/ambitious' that modifies شاه 'king', and زمین 'land' functions as the epithet 'of the land' (شاه زمین = 'king of the land/earth'). The phrase as a whole gives the source: 'from the presence of the ambitious king of the land.'
segments.s364-g7: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they went away/they departed'. هر دو 'both' specifies the subject. شده خاکسار is a resultative/participial phrase: شده (become) + خاکسار (humble, contrite one), so 'both having become humble/penitent.' The clause links an action (departure) with their resulting state.
segments.s364-g8: جهاندارشان is 'their lord/sovereign' (جهاندار = ruler) with the possessive -شان. رانده is a past participle 'driven away/expelled'. و کرده خوار pairs the causative/auxiliary کرده 'made' with the adjective خوار 'despised/contemptible' to mean 'and made (him) despicable/lowly.' Grammatically this lists two consequences of events for their sovereign.

sentences.s364-s9: They gave him that letter of the king, the chief of the spear-bearing men.
sentences.s364-s10: His scribe Marān opened the letter and read it to that magic-born king.
sentences.s364-s11: In that king's letter was written concerning the battalions and the spear-bearing men.
sentences.s364-s12: Then King Lohrasp — Gushtasp, guardian of the world, worthy of the throne.
sentences.s364-s57: Thus he went down in anger; his heart became full of blood, his eyes full of water.
sentences.s364-s58: He kept plundering, and the palace burned.
sentences.s364-s59: He brought an army into the land of Iran; everyone stood stunned, and hearts were scattered with hatred.
sentences.s364-s21: You heap hardship upon yourself, for we have thrown open the doors of the treasure.
sentences.s364-s22: I will bring from the ranks thousands upon thousands; all experienced in action, all spear‑bearers.
sentences.s364-s23: All are born of Irāj and of Pahlav — not of Afrasiab, nor of Yabghu.
sentences.s364-s24: All have kingly faces and moon‑like countenances; all are tall as cypresses and all are truthful.
sentences.s364-s45: He bestowed on them three hundred thousand chosen horsemen who had not been taken.
sentences.s364-s46: He opened the treasury and gave provisions; he struck an iron reed and set a heap upon it.
sentences.s364-s47: At that time he summoned my brother, and to him he gave one division of his army.
sentences.s364-s48: He bound us and gave another contingent; he himself went into the midst with one son.
sentences.s364-s17: We heard and saw where those words were; you were not worthy to have told them to me.
sentences.s364-s18: Neither to be hidden nor to be displayed; neither to be cast away nor to be praised.
sentences.s364-s19: You had said such things that I would bring an army to the joyful country within two months.
sentences.s364-s20: It will not be two months — nor even four from you; how would I come, like a lion on the hunt?
sentences.s364-s49: A certain Turk, infamous and wolf‑like, had passed straight on for many days.
sentences.s364-s50: He gave the army to him as spahbed; you said he knew nothing but evil.
sentences.s364-s51: When a plunderer set upon the bannerless, he gave him an elephant‑sized standard.
sentences.s364-s52: There was one called Khashash the brave; no refuge would take him, nor would the male lion go to him.
sentences.s364-s25: Some are from the king's gate at times, some from the treasure-house at times, and some (are) with caps.
sentences.s364-s26: Their world is worn away by hardship and pampering; all are lion-catchers and all are proud.
sentences.s364-s27: All are spear-bearers and sword-strikers; all are rousing (stirring) and breakers of armies.
sentences.s364-s28: When they perceive a few drumbeats bound on the elephant, their steed's hoof makes the mountain low.
sentences.s364-s37: If God wills on the day of battle, I will lay your head beneath my foot in the fight.
sentences.s364-s38: When the commander sent a messenger and the letter was read, he descended from his place and stood astonished.
sentences.s364-s39: He told his spahbed, 'At dawn tomorrow, summon the armies from the whole kingdom.'
sentences.s364-s40: The tegins of his army — the Turks of China — set out, each to every side toward the land of Turan.
sentences.s364-s33: Like the sun, with crown and throne, from their spears glory and fortune continually shine.
sentences.s364-s34: Thus they say — the spahbeds, and the select, approved mobeds.
sentences.s364-s35: You make Sihon a pulpit and Jayhun scent with musk; to us, what does it matter whether Jayhun or Sihon, even if dry?
sentences.s364-s36: They thus carry loads upon the water, so that the sun’s spring becomes dark.
sentences.s364-s29: From among them were two chosen bands of horsemen: Zarir the commander and Esfandiyar.
sentences.s364-s30: When they put on robes of iron, they set their feet toward the sun and the moon.
sentences.s364-s31: When they hang the shining mace upon their necks, splendour and exaltation shine from their maces.
sentences.s364-s32: When they stand before the host, you must keep watch over them.
sentences.s364-s41: They called his army back to him — the chiefs of the border-guards of his country.
sentences.s364-s42: His evil brother — two Ahrimans: one Kahrm and the other Andaman.
sentences.s364-s43: He ordered them to select horsemen who had not taken spoil; they chose thousand-man units of the army.
sentences.s364-s44: They gave them drums, elephants and banners, all decorated in yellow, red and purple.
sentences.s364-s5: They left Iran wearing anklets, but those anklets did not make them fortunate.
sentences.s364-s6: When from afar they saw the king's iwan, a black banner had been hoisted above his head.
sentences.s364-s7: They dismounted from their pack-beasts, broken‑hearted, and their eyes had become blind.
sentences.s364-s8: They went on foot up to his presence; their garments had turned black and their faces were pale.
sentences.s364-s13: God sent a messenger to him; all the elders came before him on foot.
sentences.s364-s14: From Arjasp the Turk, that vile, large‑eyed one — whose form was the body of an old wolf.
sentences.s364-s15: He had turned away from the good rites and religion; he chose the path of blindness and folly.
sentences.s364-s16: That base letter arrived — the one you had written to the king.
sentences.s364-s53: The army made him a sentry and set him in front; they hoisted the standard, and he became a soothsayer.
sentences.s364-s54: Another Turk, ill‑reputed and with a demon's wit, sent his message to the khedive of the Turks.
sentences.s364-s55: The keeper said, 'You, at the rear of the army; if any of us turns back onto the road...'
sentences.s364-s56: Right there where you see him, kill him for me; watch (look), so that you do not move toward that place — beware!
sentences.s364-s1: When the speech came to an end, the king of the land called the black elephant and cried out, “Bravo.”
sentences.s364-s2: He entrusted him and said to him, 'Take them; bring them from Iran and leave them at that border.'
sentences.s364-s3: The envoys of the Chinese commander came from the presence of the world-seeking king of the land.
sentences.s364-s4: They went away; both had become contrite supplicants — their sovereign had been driven off and made contemptible.
